captions.text: At exactly 11:46 PM, during a brownout in their province, nine-year-old Carlo woke up screaming that
something was biting his feet.
His mother rushed into the room with a candle.
But when she pulled off his blanket, there were no ants.
No wounds. No blood.
Just tiny, muddy handprints wrapped around both of his ankles.
To understand what happened, you need to know one thing.
Earlier that afternoon, Carlo had been playing with his cousins behind their Lola’s house.
There was an old mound of dirt under the santol tree — small, round, and covered in grass.
Their Lola always warned them: ‘Huwag niyong gagalawin ’yan. Bahay ’yan ng nuno.’
But Carlo was nine. And nine-year-olds think warnings are just adults trying to ruin fun.
So when his cousin dared him, Carlo laughed, ran up to the mound…
...and kicked it as hard as he could.
The moment his foot hit the mound, everything went quiet.
Then, from somewhere under the ground, Carlo heard
a tiny old voice whisper: ‘Hindi ka nagpaalam.’
That night, Carlo got a fever.
His right foot swelled until his slipper wouldn’t fit.
His Lola rubbed coconut oil over his skin, whispered prayers, and kept saying, ‘Say sorry before it
follows you inside.’ But
Carlo refused. He said there was nothing to apologize to.
That was when the scratching started. At
first, it came from under the bed. Then
from inside the wall. Then
from inside Carlo’s pillow. His
mother picked it up and screamed. The
pillowcase was filled with soil. Wet, black soil. And
buried inside it was Carlo’s missing slipper. The one he had lost beside the mound.
Carlo finally cried and whispered: ‘Tabi-tabi po. Sorry po.’
For a moment, the room became still.
Then a tiny voice answered from under the bed: ‘Hindi ikaw ang kailangan naming kunin.’
Carlo’s baby sister was standing inside her crib.
Smiling.
Her feet were covered in mud.
And behind her, on the wall, written by tiny muddy fingers, were three words:
‘May utang pa.’
There is still a debt.
